15 - QA 7.2.mp4
Welcome to Q&A for section 7. 2. This is all about setting your quarterly objectives to roll up to your annual goal. So you mentioned you need one person's neck you can choke. It makes sense that, given the situation, there is, like, for example, you use Jay, the video guy. That's an individual situation when you can hold him accountable. Yep. But what about day-to-day company operations? Like, what's the best position to bring in to choke out, given that you need to hold somebody accountable? Yeah, so you've got to look at when's the right hire. I mean, and that example would be a chief operating officer, would be that. And then they would have a ton of different responsibilities. So you have to look at it. It has to be tangible.
It has to be measurable. And then you find the best person on your team to assign it to. And then there's nowhere to hide. When you get one neck to choke and you put a date, then they have to row towards that. And every day you're asking about it, and you can be clear on it. What are some of the best quarterly actions to take from your past experience? I would say the ones I would literally copy and paste. If you're trying to scale, you need sales and marketing plans and execution. You need customer retention plans. You need organic customer growth plans. I mean, all of those to me are no-brainers. Depending on where the evolution you are of your agency, you may want to have a technology roadmap that you're trying to build a piece of technology.
You want to create a financial team and an operations team to ensure that your profit margins are staying at that 25% plus. So I literally, these are great kind of like placeholders. If every agency did this, they'd be in good shape. Same question, but for yearly goals. What are some of the best yearly actions to take from your past experience? Definitely the 25% plus profit. And then I think, depending on the maturity and where the company is, I would like to see 30% plus in growth. So if it's a bigger company, that's a harder number to do. But if you're a smaller company, let's say you're sub $1 million in revenue, there's no reason you can't have a 200% growth number. So the smaller you are currently in revenue, I'd like to see that goal be much bigger. OK, guys, as always, below, leave us your questions. We'll get you answers in the Facebook group, and we'll see in the next section.
